title: How Animals Communicate Across Species
gist: An Animal Behaviour review by Katie Dunkley and 57 co-authors documents that cross-species cooperation, though poorly studied, occurs across many taxa via body movements, displays, and calls, exemplified by honeyguide birds leading humans to bees' nests.

insights.claim: Cross-species communication remains poorly studied because it requires animals to interpret cues outside their own species' behavioral frameworks, yet it can be useful for finding and exploiting shared resources.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Frames an understudied but ecologically important behavior.
insights.locator: Although we often talk with our dogs

insights.claim: An Animal Behaviour review by Katie Dunkley and 57 co-authors found cross-species cooperation occurs across a wide range of taxa and settings, facilitated by body movements, visual displays, calls, and other behavioral cues.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: Establishes cross-species cooperation as widespread, not anomalous.
insights.locator: Dunkley, in collaboration with a team

insights.claim: Greater honeyguide birds use specialized chattering trills and whistles to lure humans to bees' nests, then share in the honey, a classic case of interspecies cooperation for mutual resource gain.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: A concrete, mutualistic human-animal signaling example.

insights.claim: Species cooperate either to access shared resources like food or to exchange resources for services such as protection from predators.
insights.speaker: Devin Reese
insights.so_what: A simple typology of why cross-species cooperation arises.
insights.quote: individuals coordinate their actions to access shared resources, like food, or to exchange resources for services, such as protection from predators
